result:
I want to tell you about the night the sky tore open above my grandmother’s village, not with thunder or lightning, but with a soft, deliberate ripping sound, the way expensive silk gives when you test it between your hands. I was eight, wedged between the knees of cousins I had only just met, our faces tilted up as if the moon itself had decided to unzip its skin. Nobody screamed; the quiet felt ceremonial, the hush that falls inside a church when the procession enters carrying something holy no one is allowed to touch. What slid through that slit was not spacecraft or angels, but weather—weather that had already happened. Rain from 1974 dripped onto the maize we had planted that morning. A single flake of the notorious blizzard of 1998 landed on my tongue and tasted like the dust under my bed in the city I’d left three days earlier. I understood then that time is only a coat we button wrong in the dark, that every storm keeps its own address and occasionally misdelivers itself.

Years later, when people asked why I became a cartographer of obsolete atmospheres—why I Draw maps no airline will ever accept—I told them about that night. I left out the part where my grandmother stepped out of the crowd, barefoot, apron still flecked with cassava flour, and began speaking to the seam in the sky in a language I didn’t know she knew. Her voice was low, intimate, the register you use when you coax a chicken into the pot or ask a dying child where it hurts. The tear responded; it narrowed, widened, trembled like a horse’s flank under flies. Whatever bargain she struck remains hers, but the next morning the village smelled of wet iron, and every mirror was clouded with breath that did not belong to any of us.

I kept the flake of 1998 in an empty film canister, a secret relic more sacred than any saint’s knucklebone. When school became unbearable—when recess interrogations about my “exotic” hair turned into bloodied knees—I unscrewed the lid and inhaled. The cold that leapt out was exact, a silver scalpel that trimmed away the edges of whatever moment I was trapped inside. I could stand in the corridor outside the principal’s office, lungs full of borrowed winter, and feel the corridor lengthen until the bell that would release me was decades away. I became a smuggler of temporal contraband. In my pencil case: the echo of a 1929 market day, the hush that preceded the 2003 blackout, the sigh a volcano gives just before it decides not to erupt. Each one a narcotic, each one a doorway. My report cards called me distracted; they could not see the atlas blooming behind my eyelids, towns built entirely of postponed consequences.

By seventeen I had learned that every family is a private weather station. My mother carried hailstones from the day her father left, rolling them absently between her palms while she talked on the phone. They never melted; they sang, a tiny choir of fractures. My younger brother hoarded heat waves: the week before he was born, August pressed its mouth to the city and left bruises that never yellowed. When he laughed too hard, the temperature in whatever room we occupied spiked two degrees. We were a household of unauthorized climates, hiding our anomalies in Tupperware, in jewelry boxes, in the hollowed-out cavity behind the fake VHS tape labeled “Family Christmas 1993.” We never spoke of the obligation that came with these souvenirs: the way they tugged us toward the instants they were stolen from, how we had to brace ourselves every morning against the undertow of our own small eternities.

College was a disaster of ordinary skies. I arrived with two suitcases and the film canister, determined to major in meteorology like a normal person chasing normal storms. But the lecture halls insisted that weather behaved; it conformed to laws, to fronts, to the calm narration of doppler graphics. There was no unit of measurement for the ache of a nine-year-old blizzard trapped in a dorm room radiator. I lasted three semesters, then left at 3 a.m. during a perfectly respectable thunderstorm that refused to acknowledge my correspondence. I walked south with no plan except to follow the smell of wet iron that sometimes still drifted from the sleeve of a stranger. I slept in laundromats where the spinning drums dreamed of tornadoes. I learned that every Laundromat is a border crossing; socks vanish into parallel universes, and if you press your ear to the porthole of a dryer you can hear women from 1962 arguing about Eisenhower.

In Mobile, Alabama, I met a man named Jonah who kept a thundercloud in a Mason jar. He was older, beautiful in the way of cracked leather, his left ear missing a chunk from what he called “a disagreement with 1849.” We recognized each other immediately, collared transgressors pacing the same invisible perimeter. He invited me to the back room of his pawn shop where the shelves held snow globes that had somehow reversed the transaction: the world outside was miniature and plastic, while inside the glass full-sized hurricanes raged, complete with fishing boats and radio towers and one terrified disc jockey still broadcasting. Jonah told me there were only seven people alive who knew how to fold weather small enough to trade. Five of them were dying. One had forgotten the trick out of spite. The last was teaching me now.

Training was less pedagogy than enforced nostalgia. Jonah made me swallow teaspoonfuls of gone afternoons: the last daylight before electricity reached rural Tennessee, the hush inside a 1936 movie palace when the newsreel cracked halfway through footage of the Dust Bowl. Each one settled in my stomach like conflicting weathers—warm front colliding with cold, the sky confused about which century to reflect. I vomited chronology: years came up entwined with milkweed silk, with bus tickets, with the taste of my grandmother’s porch on the day she taught me to shell peas. The peas kept reappearing, tiny green commas trying to punctuate sentences I hadn’t spoken yet. I understood this was tuition; to carry weather you must first be unsettled by your own sediment.

When I could finally keep time down without retching, Jonah showed me the Market. It meets on no schedule, in no fixed place. You receive an envelope containing a flake of paint from a forgotten barn and you know: tonight, bring your wares. That night the venue was an abandoned Six Flags whose rides had been stripped by scrap thieves, outlines of horses and rockets still galloping across the sky in rust. Traders arrived carrying thermoses of 1920s fog, cigar boxes lined with hurricane eyes, kites whose tails were braided from the final breaths of unrecorded glaciers. The currency is memory, but only the portion you can afford to lose. I watched a woman trade the smell of her mother’s hair on the first day of school for a sliver of the day the Berlin Wall fell; she tucked it behind her ear like a tropical flower and immediately forgot the color of the ribbons her mother used to buy. The transaction glowed, a soft bilateral amputation.

I had brought with me the 1998 snowflake, my oldest, most loyal contraband. I intended to swap it for the coordinates of the village tear—my grandmother’s bargain, the unanswered question stitched into my sleep. But when I opened the canister, the flake refused to leave. It clung to the plastic rim, expanding, reconstituting itself into the full storm: drifts knee-deep, cars stranded on the interstate, the governor declaring a state of emergency that would not be declared for another two years. Snow filled the derelict park, packing the roller-coaster skeletonsuntil they became alabaster monuments to winters that had not yet earned belief. Traders scattered, slipping on memory-ice, their pockets spilling monsoons and heat waves that froze mid-air into chandeliers. In the chaos I saw Jonah lock eyes with me across the midway. He was smiling the way my grandmother had smiled at the torn sky: resignation braided with wonder. Then he turned into the storm himself, dissolving the way salt recedes into meat, leaving only the echo of his boot-prints filling with premature spring.

I ran. Snow pursued, but this was my storm, forged from my exile, loyal to the terrified child who had inhaled it first. It wrapped me like a mother who had never once asked where I went at night. When I stumbled out the gate, the world was summer again, cicadas screaming at the insult. The Six Flags was simply rust, no trace of weather except the single flake now reassembled in the canister, heavier, humming. I drove east until dawn, understanding that I had been refused the coordinates—not punished, refused, the way a river refuses to flow uphill. Some doors you don’t walk through; some doors walk through you.

The years that followed were a cartography of near misses. I drew maps that plotted the pressure systems of regret, isobars of lovers who left without explanation. I sold them to therapists who hung them behind couches like abstract art. Patients cried without knowing why; sometimes whole sessions were lost in the downdraft of a single Sunday afternoon in 1986. I was paid in installments of forgotten time—an hour from someone’s first hangover, twenty minutes of post-coital languor, seconds skimmed from the moment before a car crash. I stored them in vintage milk bottles, the glass thick enough to muffle their pleading. My apartment was a museum of almost: almost happiness, almost disaster, almost the courage to call home.

One April evening I felt the tug—an insistent yank behind the sternum like a fishhook embedded in a dream. It was the direction I had avoided since the Market: south-east, toward the village I had not seen since the funeral. My grandmother had died twice. The first death was ordinary: heart, hospital, the hush of shoes on linoleum. The second happened three nights after the burial when she appeared at the foot of my bed smelling of rain that hadn’t fallen yet. She said nothing, only extended her hand; it was translucent, full of stars the size of sesame seeds. I had hidden under the covers like a child hiding from baseline astronomy. When I emerged she was gone, but the room smelled of wet iron, the same bargain scent. Since then the tug had slept inside me; now it was awake, stretching.

I drove the old pickup I had bought with a stranger’s recalled first kiss. The highway unrolled like a cassette tape whose song keeps changing genre. Somewhere past Chattanooga the sky began to rehearse the rip I remembered, a delicate tear directly overhead, edges fluttering like eyelids fighting sleep. I pulled over. Traffic blurred on either side, drivers unaware of the celestial rehearsal. The tear was smaller than memory, patient. I stepped into the meridian, weeds brushing my ankles with the urgency of beggars. Above me the slit widened enough to reveal—not past or future—but the present folded inside out: the village as it was at that exact moment, elders on cane chairs, children chasing a dog whose fur was threaded with myrrh. I saw my grandmother, alive in the eternal now the tear preserved, stirring coffee she would never finish, the spoon circling endlessly without touching porcelain. She looked up, straight at me across the braid of years, and shrugged: So you finally came back.

I understood the transaction now. The tear did not deliver you; it accepted you. I could step through, join the looping afternoon, become a ghost who aged sideways, every birthday a widening circle rather than a forward march. The price was the storm in my pocket, the accumulated weather of decades. I would enter small, carrying only the flake of 1998, and in exchange the village would absorb the rest: every bottled hour, every stolen sigh, every misdelivered monsoon. They would weather them on my behalf, metabolize them the way trees metabolize lightning—slowly, into rings, into the patience of wood. I would live forever in the hour before supper, half-heard gossip, mosquito coils burning like tiny galaxies. I would forget the world of calendars, of rent, of men who loved me only on weekdays. I would forget the way my mother’s hailstones sounded when they finally shattered against the kitchen tile the night she admitted she no longer remembered my father’s face. All of it would become humidity, the creak of a porch swing, the distance between church bells.

I almost stepped. I wanted to. But in the meridian the weeds had grown tall enough to brush my palms, and they were dripping with the resignation of real time: sticky, green, complicated. Behind me the pickup’s engine ticked itself cool, an ordinary dying sound. I thought of the patients who wept under my impossible maps, how they deserved their sorrow unmediated by secondhand storms. I thought of Jonah dissolved into salt, of the woman who traded her mother’s hair and walked away lighter, balder in memory. I thought of my brother’s August heat waves, how they might one day scorch him from the inside if no one taught him folding. If I stepped through, who would carry the lesson? Who would stand in laundromats listening for 1962? Absence, too, is weather; it needs witnesses the way lightning needs ground.

I backed away. The tear trembled, disappointed, then sealed without scab, the sky as innocent as a lie you tell to survive. I got into the truck and drove on, but I left the canister in the meridian, lid off. By morning the 1998 storm would have rejoined the atmosphere, snowing on commuters who would blame an unseasonal cold front, never knowing they were brushing against the underside of my eighth winter. I kept only a single flake, small as a freckle, tucked beneath the nail of my left thumb where it throbbed like a heartbeat out of season. A reminder that exile is portable, that every weather ever stolen still longs for the latitude it was torn from.

The village no longer appears on maps; GPS glitches politely around its coordinates the way polite people avoid mentioning a tumor. But sometimes, on nights when the barometer plummets and the air tastes of iron left out in rain, I stand on whatever rooftop I can access and extend my left hand. The flake under my nail pulses, trying to return home. I open my fist and let it try. I have no tear to offer, only the thin membrane between now and then, the bargain my grandmother struck still circulating in my blood like a rewritten thermostat. The sky stays closed. The city below me flickers, people negotiating their own small apocalypses—subway delays, breakups, the price of insulin. None of them look up. That is all right. Weather is only news when it arrives; when it refuses, it becomes biography.

I keep drawing maps, though now they are meant to fade. I mix the ink with tap water and salt, with crumbs of the afternoons I voluntarily forget. Within weeks the lines ghost themselves, leaving behind the faint braille of what once mattered. I sell them to people who need proof that disappearance can be gentle. They hang them in bathrooms, above desks where resignation letters are drafted. Over months the paper returns to blank, and the customers call asking if they dreamed the purchase. I tell them maps, like storms, have itineraries we merely borrow. I tell them the village is fine, the looped afternoon tolerable, the coffee perpetually warm. I tell them nothing about the price, how every subtracted memory leaves a scar shaped exactly like the moment you decide not to step through.

And yet, and yet—some mornings I wake with the taste of cassava on my tongue, the scrape of porch-swing chains behind my knees. The flake under my nail has grown into a miniature glacier, cold enough to hurt, translucent enough to see through: a single frame of my grandmother stirring eternity. I know that when I die—truly, no second chances—the glacier will melt, and the 1998 storm will reconstitute itself inside whatever room contains me. Nurses will speak of sudden squalls, of snow drifts against the IV stands. They will call it a medical anomaly. They will not know they are witnessing repatriation, weather finally escorted back to the moment it was hijacked, a circle closing with the politeness of a story that refuses to moralize.

Until then I practice the smaller kindnesses. I leave laundromats unlocked at night. I plant pea seeds in abandoned lots and walk away before they sprout. I stand in checkout lines behind parents whose children cry the way weather cries—without explanation, without apology—and I imagine slipping them a thimbleful of the afternoon my brother was born, humid, giddy, infinite. I never do. Innocence, once traded, cannot be re-gifted. But the imagining is its own map, lines drawn in breath on tempered glass, fading before the next customer requests cigarettes, ibuprofen, the small mercies of the un-weathered world.

If you find this—and you might, because I am leaving it in the pocket of a thrift-store coat shaped like the night the sky tore—do not look for coordinates. Do not Google the village; it has already forgotten the algorithm. Simply know that somewhere a seam persists, rehearsing its opening, waiting for the bargain that will not be struck again. Know that every storm you survive deposits a flake beneath the nail of whatever hand you use to wave goodbye. Know that the maps you buy will fade, and the memories you trade will echo in strangers’ dreams, and the sky, the sky is only a coat we button wrong in the dark. Button it anyway. The rip is coming. When it does, stand very still, extend your hand, decide what weather you can finally afford to return. The answer will be less than you think, and more than you can bear. That is all right. We are all cartographers of the obsolete, the atmosphere our only true inheritance, circulating, refusing to settle, teaching us over and over that exile is just another word for sky.

When it does, stand very still, extend your hand, decide what weather you can finally afford to return. The answer will be less than you think, and more than you can bear. That is all right. We are all cartographers of the obsolete, the atmosphere our only true inheritance, circulating, refusing to settle, teaching us over and over that exile is just another word for sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
